title: Lesson 357
transcript: Okay, let's talk about privacy. We have condensed all the information on privacy on the CISSP exam down into this one section here, this one video that we're going to go through. Privacy is obviously a very important topic. and a lot of the organizations need to spend a lot of time thinking about privacy and making sure they're properly protecting people's personal information and ensuring their compliance with various appropriate laws and regulations and security has a big role in this because there's this really well-known expression right you can't have privacy without security. The way that we achieve confidentiality of personal data, integrity, availability, and so forth is by implementing various security controls. And so security often has a very important role in relation to privacy in implementing various security controls. So let's start here with a definition of privacy. And this is a really good definition. In fact, it's probably wording verbatim that you'll see on the exam. So privacy is the state or condition of being free from being observed or disturbed by other people. And when you first read that, it seems like a bit of an odd definition. Why is that the definition of privacy? Maybe to put this into context, it's useful to think about where did this definition come from? And maybe to help you remember this, I'll give you a bit of history behind this. So there's a particular country in the world that's very well known for having excellent privacy laws. And maybe for you at home, I'll let you think about that for a second. Which country do you think has really excellent privacy laws? And when we teach this live in class, we usually get answers like, oh, Switzerland. And Switzerland does have really good privacy laws as it relates to protecting banking information, but not generally overall. But the country I'm thinking is actually just north of Switzerland. It's Germany. Germany, for many, many decades now, has been one of the leaders in privacy laws and legislation. And for a very good reason. Because Germany has a bit of an interesting history, right? I mean, during World War II, the nazi party in germany was collecting vast amounts of personal information and they were using it for absolutely horrendous and despicable purposes they were collecting information on people's gender ethnicity religion etc and they were using that information for horrendous purposes And so Germany took a pretty strong stand after World War II to say, we are not the Nazi party and these are not the beliefs of Germany as a whole. And Germany started enacting many, many privacy laws to make it very clear that you know, someone's personal information is their information and that when you read this definition now, right, the state of being conditioned for being free from being observed or disturbed by other people, you do not want a government or a company collecting your personal information and using it in a way that would disturb or harm you. And so, you know, maybe given that context, it makes it a little easier to remember this definition. And that's something really important here. Someone's personal information should belong to them. It should be their information that they have the right to protect. And others shouldn't be able to use, say, my personal information in a way that would harm or disturb me. Now, when it comes to defining exactly what is personal information, thankfully, this is beyond the scope of the CISSP exam. There's a lot of, I mean, if this was a privacy exam, we'd have to get into all this terminology here. You'd have to know the subtle and important differences between PII and PHI and so forth. And if you look at different privacy laws and regulations around the world, they all define exactly what is personal information or personal data with subtle but important differences. But thankfully, this is the CISSP exam, a security exam, not a privacy exam. So for purposes of the CISSP exam, you can essentially treat all of these terms as interchangeable. personal data, personally identifiable information, personally sensitive personal information, personal information, et cetera. These all essentially mean the same thing, which is information that can be used on its own or in combination to identify an individual. That's basically what personal information is. Information that can be used on its own or in combination to identify an individual. Now you might be wondering about what does this part mean about on its own or in combination? What that's getting at is what are known as direct versus in direct identifiers. A direct identifier is something that's on its own uniquely identifies an individual. A perfect example of a direct identifier is something like a social insurance number or a passport number or a driver's license number. These are numbers that on their own absolutely, directly, uniquely identify an individual. And typically any direct identifiers are absolutely going to be considered personal data under just about any countries or jurisdictions laws. Where things get a lot more murky is with indirect identifiers. Indirect identifiers are bits of information that on their own don't uniquely identify someone. So you can see great examples here, things like age, gender, ethnicity, someone's postal code. On their own, they don't uniquely identify someone. But when I say where it gets murky, the challenge with indirect identifiers is that if you have a lot of indirect identifiers, and especially if the population size is small enough, you can suddenly start to uniquely identify someone. So let me give you a couple of extreme examples, right? You go to New York City and you just have a couple of indirect identifiers, like someone's You're just looking for someone that's 32 years old, male, and has green eyes. You have no idea who that is in New York City. But you go to a small town of 50 people and you say, I'm looking for the person that's 32 years old, male, and has green eyes. And you're like, oh yeah, that's Bob. He just lives over there. So this is where indirect identifiers can be used to uniquely identify someone, if you have enough of them, and especially if the popularity size is small enough. And that's why certain privacy laws around the world will consider certain indirect identifiers to be personal data. And some laws and regulations even segment out and identify online identifiers, like email addresses, IP addresses, and cookies, and things like that, for example. It varies significantly, though. Don't worry, for the CISP exam, you don't have to memorize all the various subtleties between all these different privacy laws around the world. And there are a lot of privacy laws around the world. We'll get to those in a second. One important bit of terminology you have to take away from this slide. There's a lot of text on this slide. Really only one thing I want you to focus on. It's the text in yellow as usual. Data controllers. This is a really important term that you absolutely need to remember for the exam. A data controller is the owner, the person that's accountable for the protection of personal data. Certain important privacy legislation or regulations like GDPR, the European Union's privacy regime, which we'll talk about in a couple minutes here, GDPR defines this role of a data controller, and that's the exact wording they use, data controller. And what they define data controller to mean is this is the individual that is accountable for the protection of personal data. And GDPR requires that any organization that's collecting personal data have someone in the organization that has this role of data controller. Maybe it's the chief privacy officer or something like that, but someone in the organization has to have this role of a data controller. They have to be accountable for the protection of personal data. So remember that. Now, as I mentioned, there's lots of different privacy laws around the world. You don't really need to know the specifics on any of them, with one major exception, which is GDPR. We'll talk about that in a second. Now, I will mention actually one other, not just because I'm Canadian, but because you may see it on the exam, PIPEDA. PIPEDA, the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, PIPEDA is Canada's privacy law. That sometimes shows up on the exam, so just be aware that PIPEDA is Canada's law. And some of the other ones, in fact, we talked about earlier, more in relation to security control frameworks. If you recall, we talked about SOX and COPA and HIPAA. They also, in the US, those frameworks also contain some privacy components as well. But the major one that we need to talk, spend a few minutes talking about here is GDPR, the General Data Protection Regulation. This is the European Union's overarching privacy regime. And the reason that we're going to spend a few minutes talking about here is because you'll see questions, you could very well see questions about it on the exam. and really not any other privacy law or regulation. If you're wondering why, it's because GDPR is generally considered to be one of the absolute best privacy laws around the world. And what a lot of countries are currently in the process of doing is modeling their own privacy laws after GDPR. So GDPR is kind of considered sort of a global bellwether for privacy regulations. And in fact, a great example of this is California just very recently enacted their own new privacy regime, very much modeled after GDPR. An important part of GDPR is that it is an overarching privacy regime, and by that I mean it covers every sector of the European Union's... any type of organization. government organization, law firm, sole proprietorship, corporation, doesn't matter. GDPR applies. And it's for every sector of the economy, from finance to resource extraction to healthcare and everything in between. GDPR covers everything. The reason I'm highlighting this is because a lot of you will be Americans that are taking this class. America is the exact polar opposite of this. America has what's referred to as a sectorial privacy regime, which means that different sectors of the economy have different privacy laws. For example, within healthcare, you have HIPAA for just protecting the online privacy of children online. You have COPPA. So it's very fragmented and it's very kind of broken up by industry. GDPR is different. It is overarching. Now, there's a few other key bits of information you need to know and memorize about GDPR. The first is this supervisory authority, the SA. What this is, is a supervisory authority is meant to be an independent authority. body within each European Union state, so within Germany, France, Spain, Hungary, you know, every European Union state has to establish their own supervisory authority. And the role of the supervisory authority is that they will investigate any complaints, any potential privacy breaches. And so this next line here, a data subject, that's the individual, has the right to lodge a complaint with the supervisory authority. So let's say I'm a citizen of the European Union. If I say that, hey, I don't think Facebook is using, you know, or if I say, I think Facebook is using my data in a way that I find inappropriate or, you know, in violation of GDPR. Maybe I asked for them to delete all my data and they didn't, right? That's the right to be forgotten. Who would I lodge the complaint with? The supervisory authority. One more other little tidbit about GDPR that's not on this slide is how quickly an organization needs to report a privacy breach. And it is very quickly, within 72 hours, within three days. If an organization has discovered that they have a privacy breach, they are required to report that within 72 hours. And that is a very stringent requirement. And that doesn't matter where the breach was first identified. So if the breach was identified first with, say, the organization's cloud service provider, the organization still just has sorry, 72 hours in which to report the breach. So organizations have to think about this. They have to make sure that if they're outsourcing systems and data to a service provider, that the service provider will notify them, hopefully within something like 24 or 48 hours. So that gives the organization time to then report it to the regulator. Privacy breaches have to be reported within 72 hours. Now, the next piece we're going to talk about here are what are known as the OECD guidelines. The OECD stands for the Organization of Economic Cooperation and Development. This is a multinational organization that's all about trying to facilitate global commerce and trade, trying to make it easier for companies to operate around the world. And what the OECD noticed is that there's a lot of privacy laws out there and a lot of organizations were struggling with how should they structure their privacy program to try and make sure they're in compliance with all these various privacy laws around the world. And so the OECD came up with what they refer to as their privacy guidelines. A really key word here is the word guideline. If you recall, as John talked about, guidelines are suggestions, recommendations. They're not, thou shalt do this, right? They're not requirements. And that's an important thing to remember here. These are guidelines. They're recommendations. for basically to sort of suggest to an organization, here's how you might want to structure your privacy program to make it easier to be in compliance with all these various privacy laws and regulations around the world. And that's fundamentally what OECD is. It's a set of guidelines to help a company structure, have the right foundation in place for their privacy program, which will then make it a lot easier to be in compliance with the very specific laws and regulations of each country and jurisdiction. So let's take a moment here and walk through these privacy guidelines. It starts with the collection limitation principle. And this is the idea that an organization should only collect only the personal information that they need and nothing more. I mean, this is just a good idea from a security perspective. If you can minimize the amount of personal data that you have, you have to worry much less about a breach, right? No personal data, you don't have to worry about a breach at all. And so if organizations can minimize the amount of personal data that they're collecting, it makes the job of protecting that personal data a lot easier. And so the collection limitation principle is about only collecting absolutely what you need. And of course, make sure you do it lawfully and so forth. The data quality principle is about this idea that if an organization collects personal data, they're now accountable for the integrity of that data. And this is important. In certain extreme cases, the integrity of personal data can quite literally be life and death. Let me give you an extreme example. Imagine I walk into a hospital emergency room and I'm like, oh, I got a pain in my side. And the ER nurse checks me in, asks for... For instance, am I allergic to penicillin? And I say, yes, I'm allergic to penicillin. And they gather a few more bits of information, and then I go, ah, and I pass it on the floor. So they rush me into an operating room. And as part of the operation on me, they check the system to see if I'm allergic to penicillin, because they want to give me penicillin. And the system says, nope, Rob is not allergic to penicillin. And so they give me penicillin. What could the result of that be? Bad stuff and death. Exactly. I mean, this is obviously a very extreme example, but this is the idea. When an organization collects personal data, they have to maintain the accuracy, the integrity of that personal data. Next one here is the purpose specification principle. And what this is about is that an organization should define exactly why they're collecting someone's personal information. They should specify, we're collecting your personal information for X, Y, and Z purposes. And then closely tied to that is the use limitation principle, which means, of course, the organization should only use the personal data for the reasons that they've specified. Now the next one here, the security safeguards principle. This is just emphasizing what I talked about at the very beginning, which is you cannot have privacy without security. You need good security controls in place to achieve privacy. And so the security safeguards principle essentially calls that out. The openness principle, and this is a bit of a subtle one, but the openness principle is essentially about an organization's culture. If an organization is gonna be collecting and using personal information, that organization should be open and transparent and have things like a very clearly defined privacy policy and make sure that they're open and transparent with their customers and everyone else about here's why we're collecting personal information, here's what we're using it for, et cetera. Openness principle is essentially about the organization's culture. The individual participation principle is this idea that my personal data belongs to me and I should have the choice as to who I share it with and what they do with my personal information. And maybe, not maybe, but even I could even say to an organization that's collected my personal information, you know what? I don't want you to have my personal data anymore. I want you to erase all of my information. This is sometimes referred to as the right to be forgotten. And this is all sort of this core idea that it was the data subject. It's my personal data. I should be able to choose who collects it, what they use it for, and whether or not I want them to retain it anymore. And then the final principle here is the accountability principle. And this is the idea that just emphasizing that when an organization collects personal data, they are now accountable for the protection of that data, right? There has to be a data controller with accountability for the protection of the personal data. So there you go. Those are the OECD privacy guidelines. Guidelines. Recommendations. And the final thing just to close out this section on privacy here is this is why we're talking about it on a security certification, right? You cannot achieve privacy without security.
